hook: You already know how to summarize data with means and proportions — but how do you know if a difference you observe is real or just random chance? Every day, doctors, scientists, and businesses face exactly this question when deciding whether a new drug works or a marketing campaign succeeded. In this unit, you'll build the tools to answer 'Is this result meaningful, or could it just be noise?'
concept: Hypothesis testing is a formal framework for using sample data to make decisions about the world — it lets you weigh evidence against a default assumption (the 'null hypothesis') and decide, with quantified uncertainty, whether that assumption should be rejected.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn the core logic of hypothesis testing — setting up null and alternative hypotheses, understanding what it means to 'reject' a claim, and following the step-by-step structure used in every test that follows.

Apply the t-test to determine whether a sample mean provides enough evidence to conclude that a population mean differs from a specific claimed value, especially when the population standard deviation is unknown.
Master the z-test for proportions to decide whether observed sample data — like a survey response rate — is consistent with a hypothesized population proportion $p_0$.
You will learn to compare two independent groups — testing whether the difference between two means or two proportions is statistically meaningful rather than a product of sampling variability.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Type I and Type II Errors
topic_roadmap.description: Understand the two ways a hypothesis test can go wrong — falsely rejecting a true null hypothesis ($\alpha$) or failing to detect a real effect ($\beta$) — and learn how to reason about the trade-off between them.

You will learn to compute and correctly interpret the $p$-value as a measure of evidence against the null hypothesis, and apply significance thresholds to make principled, defensible decisions from data.
